---
title: "2.5 Compound Boolean Expressions"
subtitle: "Part 1: &&, ||, !, Ranges, and Truth Tables"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## And, Or, and Not

### Logical operators

What if you want two things to be true before the body of the conditional is executed?

Use `&&` as a logical <span class="term">and</span> to join two Boolean expressions. The body of the condition will only be executed if <span class="mark">both are true</span>.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: compoundAnd`

Textbook prompt: What if you want to go out and your parents say you can go out if you clean your room and do your homework?

Run the code below and try different values for `cleanedRoom` and `didHomework` and see what they have to be for it to print `You can go out`.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: compoundAnd`

```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        boolean cleanedRoom = true;
        boolean didHomework = false;
        if (cleanedRoom && didHomework)
        {
            System.out.println("You can go out");
        }
        else
        {
            System.out.println("No, you can't go out");
        }
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: compoundAnd`

Runestone checks that the original code has been changed and that `main` prints:

```text
You can go out
```

## Logical Or

### Inclusive or

What if it is okay if only one of two things is true?

Use `||` as a logical <span class="term">or</span> to join two Boolean expressions. The body of the condition will be executed if <span class="mark">one or both</span> are true.

In programming, the or-operator is an <span class="term">inclusive-or</span>: the whole expression is true if either one or the other or both conditions are true.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: compoundOr`

Textbook prompt: Your parents might say you can go out if you can walk or they don't need the car.

Try different values for `walking` and `carIsAvailable` and see what the values have to be to print `You can go out`.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: compoundOr`

```java
public class Test2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        boolean walking = false;
        boolean carIsAvailable = false;
        if (walking || carIsAvailable)
        {
            System.out.println("You can go out");
        }
        else
        {
            System.out.println("No, you can't go out");
        }
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: compoundOr`

Runestone checks that the original code has been changed and that `main` prints:

```text
You can go out
```

## Numeric Range Checks

### `||` for error ends, `&&` for in range

With numerical values, the **or** (`||`) operator is often used to check for error conditions on different ends of the number line.

The **and** (`&&`) operator is often used to see if a number is in a range.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: compound-number-range`

Textbook prompt: Explore how `&&` and `||` are used with numbers below. Try different values for `score` like `-10` and `110` in the code below.

```java
public class TestNum
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int score = 10; // Try -10 and 110
        if (score < 0 || score > 100)
        {
            System.out.println("Score has an illegal value.");
        }
        if (score >= 0 && score <= 100)
        {
            System.out.println("Score is in the range 0-100");
        }
    }
}
```

Runestone checks that the original code has been changed.

## Not Operator

### `!` negates a Boolean value

The <span class="term">not</span> (`!`) operator can be used to negate a Boolean value.

We've seen `!` before in `!=` (not equal). If you use `!` in expressions with `&&` and `||`, be careful because the results are often the opposite of what you think it will be at first.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: not-operator`

Textbook prompt: The code below says if homework is not done, you can't go out. Try different values for `homeworkDone`.

```java
public class TestNot
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        boolean homeworkDone = false;
        if (!homeworkDone)
        {
            System.out.println("Sorry, you can't go out!");
        }
    }
}
```

Runestone checks that the original code has been changed.

## Operator Precedence

### `!`, then `&&`, then `||`

In Java, `!` will be executed before `&&`, and `&&` will be executed before `||`, unless there are parentheses.

Anything inside parentheses is executed first.

## Truth Table

### `P && Q`

An expression involving logical operators like `P && Q` evaluates to a Boolean value, `true` or `false`.

::: {.table-fit}
| P | Q | P && Q |
|---|---|---|
| `true` | `true` | `true` |
| `false` | `true` | `false` |
| `true` | `false` | `?` |
| `false` | `false` | `false` |
:::

The result of `P && Q` is only true if both `P` and `Q` are true.

## Truth Table Checkpoint

### `fillintheblank:: truthTableFill`

Textbook prompt: The truth table above is missing one result. What is the result of `P && Q` when `P=true` and `Q=false`?

Correct response: `false`.

Reasoning: Both values must be true for `&&` to return true.

## Truth Table

### `P || Q`

The result of `P || Q` is true if either `P` or `Q` is true. It is also true when both of them are true.

::: {.table-fit}
| P | Q | P || Q |
|---|---|---|
| `true` | `true` | `true` |
| `false` | `true` | `?` |
| `true` | `false` | `true` |
| `false` | `false` | `false` |
:::

## Truth Table Checkpoint

### `fillintheblank:: truthTableFill2`

Textbook prompt: The truth table above is missing one result. What is the result of `P || Q` when `P=false` and `Q=true`?

Correct response: `true`.

Reasoning: Only one of the two has to be true with `||`, so this will print true.

## Trace Practice

### Compound Boolean expressions

For each case, choose `first case` or `second case`.

```java
if (x > 0 && (y / x) == 3)
{
   System.out.println("first case");
}
else
{
   System.out.println("second case");
}
```

1. `x = 3`, `y = 9`
2. `x = 3`, `y = 6`

## Trace Answers

### `mchoice:: traceCompoundBools`, `traceCompoundBools2`

1. Correct answer: `first case`.
   Reasoning: both conditions are true.

2. Correct answer: `second case`.
   Reasoning: the second condition is false because `6 / 3 == 2`.

## Trace Practice

### `mchoice:: qcbc_or`

What is printed when the following code executes and `x` has been set to `3` and `y` has been set to `6`?

```java
if (x > 0 || (y / x) == 3)
{
   System.out.println("first case");
}
else
{
   System.out.println("second case");
}
```

Correct answer: `first case`.

Reasoning: the first condition is true, even though the second one is not.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- state that `A && B` is true only when both sides are true
- state that `A || B` is true when either side or both sides are true
- use `||` for out-of-range error checks and `&&` for in-range checks
- use `!` to negate a Boolean value
- apply Java precedence: `!`, then `&&`, then `||`
- complete truth-table entries before tracing code
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 2

[Next: Short Circuit and Truth Tables POGIL →](2-5-part-2-short-circuit-and-truth-table-pogil.html)
